title: How the brain encodes implicit memory without your permission
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your body keeps score without asking permission 

 I was sitting in my therapist's office last year, describing a panic attack I'd had while folding laund...
htmlContent:
Your body keeps score without asking permission

I was sitting in my therapist's office last year, describing a panic attack I'd had while folding laundry. Not while navigating a car accident or giving a presentation. Folding laundry. A quiet Tuesday afternoon. And suddenly my heart was pounding, my vision narrowed, and I was back in a moment I hadn't thought about in years.

Here's the thing. My conscious mind had no idea what was happening. But my body knew. My nervous system knew. And it had been storing that memory for decades without telling me.

That's implicit memory. It's the memory your brain encodes without your permission, without your awareness, and often without any narrative attached. It's not a story you can tell. It's a sensation, a posture, a flinch, a sudden drop in your stomach. It's the way your shoulders tense when someone raises their voice. The way your throat closes when you smell a particular cologne. The way your chest tightens when someone stands too close to your left side.

Bessel van der Kolk spent decades studying this phenomenon. His work at the Trauma Center in Boston showed that trauma isn't stored in the narrative parts of the brain. It's stored in the amygdala, the brainstem, the body itself. His book What Happened to You? (paid link) offers a compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many. But the science behind it is clear: your brain doesn't ask if you're ready to remember. It just remembers. Automatically. Implicitly.

And that's the part that drives people crazy. Because you didn't choose this. You didn't sit down and decide, "I'm going to encode this moment of helplessness so that twenty years from now, I can have a full-body panic attack while buying groceries." No. Your brain did it for you. As a survival mechanism. Without your consent.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets interesting.

The brain's silent filing system

Think about how you learned to ride a bike. You didn't read a manual. You didn't memorize a sequence of steps. You got on, fell off, got back on, and eventually your body just knew. You can't explain how you balance on two wheels. You just can. That's implicit memory working in your favor.

Now think about what happens when your nervous system encounters a threat. Your brain's hippocampus, the part that timestamps and contextualizes memories, can go offline during intense stress. This is what happens during trauma. The event gets encoded, but without a date stamp, without a "this happened then and is over now" marker. It gets stored as raw sensory data. A smell. A sound. A body position. A feeling of being trapped.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, calls this "unfinished biological business." Your body prepared to fight or flee. But you couldn't. You froze. And that incomplete action pattern gets locked into your nervous system. It doesn't fade with time. It waits. It watches. And it activates when something in your environment matches the original threat, even if that match is subtle. Even if you don't consciously notice it.

I had a client once who couldn't tolerate the sound of a microwave beeping. Not a loud sound. Not a threatening sound. But every time it beeped, her heart raced and she felt a wave of nausea. It took months of work to trace it back to a hospital waiting room when she was eight years old, waiting for news about her mother. The microwave in that waiting room beeped every time someone reheated coffee. She didn't remember the waiting room. She didn't remember the beeping. But her body remembered. Her brain had encoded that sound as a signal of impending bad news. Without her permission. Without her memory. Just pure, raw, implicit association.

Wild, right?

This is why talk therapy alone often falls short for trauma. You can talk about your childhood for years. You can understand intellectually why you react the way you do. But your implicit memory doesn't respond to intellectual understanding. It responds to the body. To sensation. To movement. To breath. You can't reason your way out of a memory that was never encoded in words.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine himself. It walks through how the body holds and releases these implicit memories.

The hippocampus goes offline, the amygdala stays online

Here's the neuroscience, simplified but not dumbed down.

Your brain has two main memory systems. Explicit memory is what you think of as memory: facts, events, stories you can tell. "I went to the beach in 2005." "My grandmother made lasagna on Sundays." This system depends on the hippocampus, a seahorse-shaped structure deep in your brain.

Implicit memory is everything else. How to tie your shoes. How to recognize a face. How to feel afraid when you hear a particular tone of voice. This system runs through the amygdala, the cerebellum, and the body itself. It doesn't need the hippocampus at all.

So what happens during trauma? Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed that your nervous system has a hierarchy of responses. First, social engagement. Then, fight or flight. Then, shutdown and freeze. When you're in the middle of a traumatic event, your brain prioritizes survival over memory encoding. The hippocampus, which requires a lot of energy and processing time, can go partially offline. The amygdala, which is fast and automatic, stays fully online.

This means the event gets encoded in your implicit system but not your explicit system. You don't have a clear story about what happened. But you have a body that reacts. You have a nervous system that triggers. You have feelings that seem to come from nowhere.

Dan Siegel, the interpersonal neurobiology researcher, calls this "integration failure." The different parts of your brain aren't talking to each other. The implicit memory is there, but it's not linked to explicit awareness. It runs your behavior from the basement, and you don't even know it's there.

This is why you can have a panic attack folding laundry. Your brain detected something in that moment that matched an implicit memory. Maybe the angle of the light. Maybe the texture of the fabric. Maybe the way your body was positioned. The amygdala said "danger" and activated your survival responses. Your conscious mind said "I'm folding towels" and couldn't understand why you were shaking.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the nervous system's ability to down-regulate after activation. It's not a cure, but it can help take the edge off while you're doing deeper work.

How implicit memory shows up in daily life

You might not think you have implicit memories causing problems. But look at your patterns. Look at what you avoid. Look at what makes you irritable for no clear reason. Look at the relationships that feel familiar even when they're not good for you.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who integrates Internal Family Systems with neuroscience, says that implicit memory often shows up as "emotional flashbacks." You don't relive the event. You just feel the feeling. Suddenly you're terrified, or ashamed, or enraged, and you don't know why. The feeling seems to come from the present moment, but it's actually coming from the past. Your brain just didn't label it as past.

I used to get unreasonably angry when my partner would ask me what I wanted for dinner. Not angry at her. Angry at the question. It felt like pressure. Like I was being tested. Like there was a right answer and I was going to get it wrong. That feeling came from somewhere. It came from a childhood where my preferences were dismissed or punished. My brain encoded "being asked what you want = danger" without ever forming a conscious memory of the specific incidents. It just created the pattern. The implicit rule. The automatic response.

Here's what's hard to hear: your brain is not trying to hurt you. It's trying to protect you. It encoded that memory because it believed, correctly or not, that the information would help you survive. The problem is that the encoding happened without context, without updating, without awareness. Your brain is running an old operating system on new hardware. It's applying solutions that were designed for a situation that no longer exists.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who applies Polyvagal Theory in practical, accessible ways. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about learning to notice what your nervous system is doing and gently guiding it toward regulation.

What helps when your brain won't ask permission

You can't erase implicit memory. That's not how the brain works. But you can create new experiences that update the old ones. This is called reconsolidation, and it's one of the most hopeful findings in neuroscience.

When you retrieve a memory, even an implicit one, it becomes temporarily malleable. For a brief window, you can add new information to it. You can pair the old trigger with a new experience of safety. You can let your body learn, in real time, that this moment is not that moment.

This is why somatic work is so powerful. You don't just talk about the memory. You let your body feel the activation, and then you help it complete the unfinished response. You let your legs run. You let your hands push. You let your throat open to scream. Not in the past. In the present. Your nervous system finally gets to do what it couldn't do then. And that changes the implicit memory.

Pat Ogden, the creator of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, emphasizes that the body's movement patterns are themselves a form of memory. You don't have to talk about what happened. You can work with how your body is organizing itself right now. You can notice the tension, the collapse, the bracing. You can track the impulse to move. And slowly, carefully, you can let your body find a new way of being in the world.

This work is slow. It's not linear. You'll have days where you feel like you're making progress and days where you're back in the same old patterns. That's normal. That's how the nervous system works. It's not a failure. It's the process.

Gabor Mate says that trauma is not what happens to you, it's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. The implicit memory is not the event itself. It's the wound left behind. And wounds can heal. Not because you erase what happened. But because you integrate it. You bring it into awareness. You let your brain finally connect the implicit to the explicit. You let yourself know what your body has known all along.

And that's the work. That's the slow, messy, unglamorous work of recovering from a brain that encoded memories without your permission. You don't have to do it alone. You don't have to do it perfectly. You just have to keep showing up, keep noticing, keep letting your body learn that it's safe now.

Even if it doesn't feel that way yet.

Even if folding laundry still makes your heart race some days.

That's okay. Your brain is just trying to protect you. And you can learn to work with it, not against it.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma by Bessel van der Kolk and Oprah Winfrey
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of body-based healing from trauma
  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A supplement that supports nervous system regulation
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to working with your nervous system
